params.text:
What to do if you witness discrimination

You have a duty not only to avoid discriminating yourself, but also to speak up when you see others treating someone unfairly. Staying silent allows poor practice to continue and lets people down.

If you witness discrimination, harassment or victimisation:

  • Support the person affected – check they are safe and make sure their dignity is protected.
  • Challenge it appropriately if it is safe to do so – sometimes a calm, respectful word is enough to stop it.
  • Report it to your manager or supervisor, following your workplace policy.
  • Record the facts accurately, clearly and without opinion.
  • Follow whistleblowing or safeguarding procedures if the concern is serious or is not acted upon.

Reporting a genuine concern is protected – you should never be victimised for raising it. Promoting equality is everyone's responsibility, every day.

params.question: You overhear a colleague repeatedly mocking a resident's accent, and the resident looks upset. What should you do?

answers.text: Check the resident is okay, challenge the behaviour if safe, and report it to your manager following workplace policy.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Supporting the person, challenging poor practice and reporting through the right channels are all part of your duty.

answers.text: Ignore it, as it is a private matter between the colleague and resident.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is harassment, not a private matter. Staying silent allows discrimination to continue.

answers.text: Join in gently so the resident does not feel singled out.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This makes you part of the harassment and further undermines the resident's dignity.

answers.text: Wait several weeks to see whether it happens again before doing anything.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Delay leaves the resident exposed to further harm. Concerns should be acted on promptly.

params.question: A care worker who reports discrimination in good faith is protected and should not be treated badly for raising the concern.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Actually this is true – treating someone badly for raising a genuine concern is victimisation and is against the law.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Punishing someone for raising a genuine concern would itself be victimisation, which is unlawful under the Equality Act 2010.
